coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and ratiometric detection of nitrite in food based on upconversion-carbon dots nanosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yaqing
Wei, Simin
Wang, Jialin
Li, Junjie
Tang, Jinlu
Aaron, Albert Aryee
Cai, Qiyong
Wang, Ningning
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite (NO2 −) is extensively found in the daily dietary environment. However, consuming too much NO2 − can pose serious health risks. Thus, we designed a NO2 −-activated ratiometric upconversion luminescence (UCL) nanosensor which could realize NO2 − detection via the inner filter effect (IFE) between NO2 −-sensitive carbon dots (CDs) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs). Due to the exceptional optical properties of UCNPs and the remarkable selectivity of CDs, the UCL nanosensor exhibited a good response to NO2 −. By taking advantage of NIR excitation and ratiometric detection signal, the UCL nanosensor could eliminate the autofluorescence thereby increasing the detection accuracy effectively. Additionally, the UCL nanosensor proved successful in detecting NO2 − quantitatively in actual samples. The UCL nanosensor provides a simple as well as sensitive sensing strategy for NO2 − detection and analysis, which is anticipated to extend the utilization of upconversion detection in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Nitrite
Ratiometric response
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CDs and Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs
The sensing mechanism of the nanosensor for NO2 −
Optimization of the NO2 − detection conditions
Sensitivity and selectivity of the nanosensor
Detection of NO2 − in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22004111 , 21974125 ), the Tackle Key Problems in Science and Technology Project of Henan Province, China ( 222102310386 ), Henan Postdoctoral Foundation ( 202002009 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M682327 ), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002 ), and the 111 Project ( D20003 , CXJD2021001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A), NaYF4:Yb,Tm (B), and NaYF4:Yb,Tm@NaYF4 (C). The inset picture in Fig. 1 A: high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and size distribution of CDs. (D) XRD patterns of NaYF4:Yb,Tm and NaYF4:Yb,Tm@NaYF4.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs, CDs + NO2 −, and NO2 −, as well as UCL emission spectra of Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs (solid purple line). The upconversion emission spectra (B) and UV–vis absorption spectra (C) of Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs, Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs + CDs, and Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs + CDs + NO2 −. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The effect of CDs concentrations on the UCL344/UCL475 ratio of Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs. (B) The effect of CDs concentrations on the UCL344/UCL475 variation of Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs. (C) The UCL344/UCL475 ratio of UCL nanosensor in buffer with different pH responses to NO2 −. (D) The UCL344/UCL475 ratio of UCL nanosensor versus time following the addition of 100 μM NO2 −. Inset: the UCL emission spectra of UCL nanosensor versus time after adding 100 μM NO2 −. The concentration of Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs used in optimization experiment was 0.04 mg mL−1.

(A) The upconversion emission spectra of UCL nanosensor at various concentrations of NO2 −. (B) The UCL344/UCL475 ratio of UCL nanosensor at various concentrations of NO2 −. Inset: the linear curve and equation of UCL nanosensor response to NO2 −. (C) NO2 − selective and anti-interference detection based on ratio UCL signal. NO2 − was present at concentrations of 200 μM, whereas other interfering ions were present at a concentration of 1 mM.
Schematic illustration of the ratiometric UCL nanosensor design for NO2 − detection in food.
Sample detection results of UCL nanosensor and Commercial kit.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and ratiometric detection of nitrite in food based on upconversion-carbon dots nanosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Medical Sciences, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Basic Medical Sciences, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite (NO2 −) is extensively found in the daily dietary environment. However, consuming too much NO2 − can pose serious health risks. Thus, we designed a NO2 −-activated ratiometric upconversion luminescence (UCL) nanosensor which could realize NO2 − detection via the inner filter effect (IFE) between NO2 −-sensitive carbon dots (CDs) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs). Due to the exceptional optical properties of UCNPs and the remarkable selectivity of CDs, the UCL nanosensor exhibited a good response to NO2 −. By taking advantage of NIR excitation and ratiometric detection signal, the UCL nanosensor could eliminate the autofluorescence thereby increasing the detection accuracy effectively. Additionally, the UCL nanosensor proved successful in detecting NO2 − quantitatively in actual samples. The UCL nanosensor provides a simple as well as sensitive sensing strategy for NO2 − detection and analysis, which is anticipated to extend the utilization of upconversion detection in food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −), which is commonly found in fertilizer, preservative, and food additive, has been widely distributed in human daily life and circumstances [1]. However, if water, crops, and foods contain excessive NO2 −, it will seriously endanger human health when absorbed by the human body [2,3]. The oxygen transport function of hemoglobin will be affected by excessive NO2 −, resulting in methemoglobinemia due to insufficient oxygen transport of hemoglobin [4]. Moreover, NO2 − has the ability to react with amines and other substances in the body to produce carcinogenic nitrosamines which can cause cancers in the stomach and esophagus [5,6]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore a simple strategy for monitoring and quantifying the level of NO2 −.

Up to now, several methods such as chromatography [7,8], electrochemistry [9–11], colorimetry [12–14], and fluorometry [15–17] have been designed for the detection of NO2 −. Although chromatography has high speed and enough sensitivity for NO2 − determination, it requires a tedious sample pre-treatment process, high operating skills, and high cost. Electrochemical analysis possesses high sensitivity and wide measurement range, but its selectivity and stability are still being bottlenecks. In terms of colorimetry, it is simple to operate, but there are still some problems with the sensitivity and anti-interference capability. Among the above analytical methods, fluorescence spectroscopy is considered one of the most promising analytical technologies in terms of simple manipulation, high sensitivity, fast response, low cost, and appreciable specificity [18–20]. As a kind of fluorescence sensor, carbon dots (CDs) are attractive due to their excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, high photobleaching resistance, low toxicity, and low cost [21–23]. To date, several groups have devised a number of CDs to detect NO2 − [24–26]. However, most of the CDs suffered a short excitation wavelength which can produce autofluorescence interference and result in a low signal-to-background ratio. Additionally, many CDs use a single emission intensity as the signal output, making it difficult to eliminate the interference of environmental factors. As a result, it is critical to design a new approach to overcome the application limitations of traditional CDs.

Recently, luminescent nanosensing probes based on upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have received abundant attention from researchers. UCNPs are able to absorb near-infrared (NIR) excitation light and convert it into UV/visible emission [27,28]. On account of the special upconversion luminescence (UCL) mechanism, UCNPs exhibit various special advantages, including low auto-fluorescence signal, high photostability, and significant anti-stokes shifts [29]. In addition, because of their numerous and sharp emission peaks [30], UCNPs become an attractive ratiometric nanoprobe for sensing. Ratiometric nanosensors are able to reduce variability brought on by environmental influence by containing a built-in correction, hence boosting sensitivity and accuracy. As an ideal nanosensor candidate, UCNPs-based nanosensors have been widely developed to detect a variety of substances, such as small molecules [31,32], anionic [33], and metal ions [34,35].

Herein, we report a ratiometric UCL nanosensor with excellent sensitivity and selectivity for NO2 − sensing. As shown in Scheme 1 , the UCL nanosensor was composed of NO2 −-responsive CDs and Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs (NaYF4:Yb,Tm@NaYF4 core-shell UCNPs). CDs can combine with NO2 − to produce an intense absorption peak around 353 nm which overlaps with upconversion emission at 344 nm, resulting in the energy to be transferred from Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs to CDs and the reduction in the intensity of UCL344 emission. In contrast, the UCL475 signal remained unchanged. Thus, the UCL344/UCL475 ratio of the UCL nanosensor increased with the concentration of NO2 −, allowing for ratiometric analysis of NO2 − in domestic water and food.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained with a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a FEI TalosF200S microscope (Thermo Scientific, USA). A Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (USA) was used for Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) spectra. An X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Almelo, Netherlands) was used for X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis. UCL spectra were taken on a FLS 980 fluorometer (Edinburgh, England). A U–3900H spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was utilized to record UV–vis absorption spectra. A Malvern ZSE zen3700 (England) was used for zeta potential measurements. The pH values of the solution were adjusted using a Mettler-Toledo Delta 320 pH meter (USA).

Yttrium chloride (YCl3) and ytterbium chloride (YbCl3) were acquired from Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd. Thulium chloride (TmCl3) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. 1-octadecene (1-ODE) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. Citric acid (CA), oleic acid (OA), and p-phenylenediamine were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. All experiments used ultrapure (UP) water that had been purified using a Milli-Q water system (Millipore, USA).

The oleic acid-capped Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs (20 mg) (the preparation process is detailed in Supporting Information) were added to a mixture solution (water/ethanol = 1:1) which contains 0.05 M of HCl. The solution was next stirred for 2 h after being ultrasonically treated for 10 min. The resulting Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs were centrifuged and repeatedly rinsed with ultrapure water. Lastly, the obtained hydrophilic Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs were redispersed with ultrapure water.

The NO2 − detection was done in acetic acid buffer (pH 3.5). Briefly, both the hydrophilic Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs and CDs solutions (75 μg mL−1) (the preparation process is detailed in Supporting Information) were mixed in a centrifuge tube to prepare the UCL nanosensor. Subsequently, a series of samples were acquired by introducing NO2 − solution to the fabricated nanosensor, each with a different concentration. The final NO2 − level ranged from 0 μM to 500 μM. The upconversion emission spectra were measured using a 980 laser in the range of 300–550 nm after 25 min reaction at room temperature. All test results were averages of three independent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM image of the CDs reveals a relatively uniform diameter of 4.3 nm (Fig. 1 A). The lattice spacing of CDs was around 0.2 nm (inset in Fig. 1A), which was attributed to the (100) facet of graphene. Furthermore, FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS were utilized to investigate the functional groups of the CDs. From the analysis of FT-IR spectra, typical stretching vibrations of C–O–C (1129 cm−1), C–N (1397 and 1264 cm−1), C=N (1516 cm−1), C=C (1628 cm−1), N/O–H (3403 cm−1) as well as the bending vibrations of N–H (828 cm−1) were confirmed (Fig. S1) [24,26]. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S2A) showed that the prepared CDs contained 81.19% C, 1.55% N, and 17.26% O, indicating that C, N, and O are the primary components of the CDs. The high resolution of C 1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) shows three peaks at 288.6 eV (C=N/C=O), 286.1 eV (C–O/C–N), and 284.6 eV (C=C/C–C) whereas that for the N 1s spectrum was composed of two peaks at 400.3 and 398.9 eV, which correspond to C=N and C–N, respectively (Fig. S2C). Similarly, two peaks at 531.7 and 533.0 eV in the fine spectra of O1s (Fig. S2D) belong to C–OH/C–O–C and C=O bonds, respectively.

Next, TEM images and XRD spectra were utilized to characterize the UCNPs. As manifested in Fig. 1B and C, the OA-capped UCNPs showed spherical shape and dispersed with an average diameter. The core nanoparticles (NaYF4:Yb,Tm) possessed 25.7 nm in diameter on average. Additionally, the size of core-shell nanoparticles (NaYF4:Yb,Tm@NaYF4) was increased to 33.8 nm. Notably, the UCL emission intensity of core-shell UCNPs was significantly improved due to the avoidance of surface-induced quenching of core UCNPs (Fig. S3). As illustrated in the XRD patterns, the peak locations of the UCNPs are highly consistent with NaYF4 (JCPDS 16–0334), which proved that they are both hexagonal phase (Fig. 1D). Given that the OA-capped UCNPs are not conducive to further applications, the OA ligand was removed from the UCNP surface by protonation effect in a hydrochloric acid solution. The removal of OA was demonstrated by FT-IR spectra in Fig. S4. This was supported by the fact that after acid treatment, the bands of –CH2- (2927 and 2855 cm−1) and -COO- (1562 and 1461 cm−1) in OA vanished.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of the nanosensor for NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 A, no UV–vis absorption peak for NO2 − (100 μM) from 250 to 600 nm was observed. When CDs were mixed with NO2 − under acidic conditions, the absorption peak at ∼390 nm disappeared, with an accompanying appearance of a new absorption at 353 nm. In addition, the zeta potential of CDs + NO2 − was −0.68 mV, while that of NO2 − and CDs were −2.56 and 4.65 mV, respectively (Fig. S5). Notably, the reaction solution of CDs and NO2 − immediately changed from colorless to orange red and showed a new absorption at 480 nm after adding N-(1-naphthyl)ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NED), while no apparent color changes were observed in the solution with only CDs or NO2 − (Fig. S6). These result indicated that new substances (diazonium salt) had been formed due to the NO2 − interact with the groups on the surface of CDs under acidic conditions [36,37]. As exhibited in Scheme 1, the inner filter effect (IFE) served as the foundation for the NO2 − sensing nanosensor. The Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs act as luminescence donors, while the NO2 −-responsive CDs as quenchers. In this design, Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs showed two emission peaks at 344 nm and 475 nm. The groups on the CDs surface bound to NO2 − can produce a new substance, resulting in an increased absorption peak around 353 nm that overlaps with the upconversion emission at 344 nm, leading to energy transfer from Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs to CDs and quenching the emission (344 nm) (Fig. 2A and B). In the presence of NO2 −, the UCL nanosensor also produced an absorption peak at 353 nm (Fig. 2C). Therefore, UCL344 can be used as a response signal and UCL475 can be used as a reference signal. As a result, the UCL344/UCL475 ratio of the UCL nanosensor was used as the output signal to realize NO2 − detection. Furthermore, hydrophilic Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs had a zeta potential of ∼11.8 mV, while the zeta potential of NO2 −-responsive CDs was ∼4.6 mV (Fig. S7A). The Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs and CDs were too far apart for the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism to take place because of electrostatic repulsion. This was corroborated by the luminescence decay curves of the Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs (Figs. S7B and C) which demonstrated that IFE should be the quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the NO2 − detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: First, the response of CDs to NO2 − was verified. Fig. S8 showed that under acidic conditions, the absorption of CDs increased significantly as NO2 − concentration increased. Then the reaction kinetics and response performance of CDs and NO2 − were investigated. The absorbance peak of CDs solution at 353 nm increased steadily with time after the addition of NO2 −, and the reaction time was about 25 min (Figs. S9A and B). The absorption peak of CDs at 353 nm progressively increased as the level of NO2 − rose from 0 μM to 500 μM until it eventually slowly reached equilibrium (Figs. S10A and B). In order to obtain better experimental results, the effects of CDs concentration, pH, and reaction time were also investigated. By fixing the concentration of NO2 − and Yb3+/Tm3+-doped UCNPs to explore the effect of CDs concentration. Fig. 3 A and B showed that the UCL344/UCL475 ratio gradually decreased as CDs concentration rose after adding NO2 −. When CDs concentration reached 75 μg mL−1, the change of the UCL344/UCL475 ratio was found to have plateaued. As a result, CDs concentration of 75 μg mL−1 was selected and used in the subsequent experiments. Furthermore, the change of intensity ratio UCL344/UCL475 of the UCL nanosensor with different NO2 − concentrations under different pH conditions was investigated (Fig. 3C). The intensity ratio reached the maximum at pH 3.5. Considering that the strong acid environment interfered with the ability to detect the real sample, the acetic acid buffer solution with pH 3.5 was used as the assay system. Besides the influence of the above factors on the UCL nanosensor, Fig. 3D displays the dynamic response of the UCL nanosensor to NO2 −. After adding 100 μM NO2 −, the UCL344/UCL475 ratio of the UCL nanosensor was stable after 25 min. Therefore, the reaction time was set at 25 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the nanosensor to NO2 − was investigated further after the optimal experimental conditions were determined. The luminescence intensity of the UCL nanosensor at 344 nm declined as NO2 − concentration increased while remaining unchanged at 475 nm (Fig. 4 A). The luminescence intensity ratio for NO2 − response in Fig. 4B showed a good linear fit from 1 μM to 20 μM (R2 = 0.9864). Using the equation LOD = 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of three groups of blank experiments, and S is the slope of the linear equation), the LOD of the UCL nanosensor was determined to be 0.25 μM. Importantly, compared to other NO2 − detection techniques that have been previously reported, this nanosensor had a better detection range and a lower detection limit (Table S1). The World Health Organization (WHO) sets the maximum limit of NO2 − in drinking water at 3.0 mg L−1 (or 65 μM), and the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) sets the maximum contaminant level of NO2 − at 21.7 μM [38]. In China, the national standard stipulates that the maximum amount of NO2 − in food is 0.15 g kg−1 (GB2760-2014). Thus, the linear range and sensitivity of this nanosensor can be suitable for the actual sample detection.

To further explore the selectivity of the UCL nanosensor for NO2 − detection in complex environments, possible interfering substances (including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Co2+, CO3 2-, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2-, HCO3 −, Cl−, I−, Br−, F−, SO4 2-) were added to the reaction system to test their influence on the detection. It can be seen from Fig. 4C that the luminescence intensity ratio is basically unchanged after adding the above ions. At the same time, NO2 − was added for the anti-interference experiment in the presence of interfering substances. The results exhibited that irrespective of the presence or absence of these interfering ions, the luminescence intensity ratio of UCL344/UCL475 decreased as NO2 − was added. Therefore, the UCL nanosensor showed outstanding selectivity and strong anti-interference capability for NO2 − detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 − in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The content of NO2 − in actual samples (i.e. tap water, lake water, sausage, and pickle water) was examined to further verify the feasibility and accuracy of the UCL nanosensor. In these investigations, the recoveries were detected by adding various concentrations of NO2 − into the real samples, and the detection findings were thoroughly compared with the commercial kit approach. As displayed in Table 1 , the average spiked recoveries of the spiked samples ranged from 90.3% to 103.8% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) under 7.3% (n = 3). All of the data collected showed that the UCL nanosensor can be utilized to accurately and reliably assess NO2 − level in complex systems.

In conclusion, an upconversion-based ratiometric nanosensor has been successfully prepared for NO2 − sensing. The addition of NO2 − caused the change in luminescence intensity ratio, and the LOD was as low as 0.25 μM. At the same time, the UCL nanosensor had great selectivity and anti-interference capability. By utilizing NIR excitation and ratiometric detection signals, the UCL nanosensor can remove the autofluorescence, increasing the detection accuracy effectively. Moreover, the reliability of the UCL nanosensor was demonstrated by determining NO2 − content in real samples and comparing it to a commercial kit approach. The designed UCL nanosensor had the advantages of simple design, high sensitivity and selectivity, which may provide a new way for food quality control in the future.

Yaqing Yang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Simin Wei: Data curation. Jialin Wang: Investigation. Junjie Li: Investigation. Jinlu Tang: Methodology. Albert Aryee Aaron: Writing – review & editing. Qiyong Cai: Investigation, Supervision. Ningning Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Zhaohui Li: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004111, 21974125), the Tackle Key Problems in Science and Technology Project of Henan Province, China (222102310386), Henan Postdoctoral Foundation (202002009), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M682327), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002), and the 111 Project (D20003, CXJD2021001).
